title: Gammon with Parsley Sauce
subtitle: Poached gammon in a thick, herb-bright bechamel
excerpt: Gammon poached in water with aromatics until completely tender, served with a thick parsley-laden bechamel. The cooking liquid becomes a light broth as it absorbs the salt and flavour from the gammon; use it as the base for the sauce rather than milk alone for more depth. A generous hand with the parsley is what distinguishes a good version from a bland one.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: boiled ham with parsley sauce. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg #35075: boiled leg of pork with parsley sauce. Classic British poached gammon dish.

content.text: Modern gammon is milder and less salty than older cured joints, but soaking an unsmoked piece is still worth doing. The poaching liquid is well-flavoured and not as salty as the gammon. Using part of it in the parsley sauce gives the sauce more depth than milk alone.

items.name: Gammon joint
items.prepNote: soaked overnight in cold water if unsmoked

items.name: Sea salt flakes
items.prepNote: taste before adding; the gammon liquid may be enough

content.text: Place the {{gammon-joint}} gammon in a large saucepan and cover with cold water. Bring to the boil, then drain and refill with fresh cold water. Add the {{onion}} onion, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{black-pepper}} peppercorns. Bring to the boil, reduce to a gentle simmer, and cook for 20 minutes per 500 g, approximately 50 minutes for a 1.2 kg piece. The gammon is done when a probe reads 75°C at the thickest point. Remove from the cooking liquid and rest for 10 minutes. Reserve 200 ml of the cooking liquid.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir for 1 minute. Gradually whisk in {{whole-milk}} of warm milk and the reserved 200 ml gammon cooking liquid, alternating additions. Cook over a medium heat, stirring constantly, until the sauce thickens and bubbles. Stir in the {{parsley-flat}} parsley. Taste before adding {{sea-salt-flakes}}: the gammon liquid is already salty. Slice the gammon and serve with the parsley sauce poured over.

rows.fix: Soaking overnight in cold water, changing the water once, significantly reduces the salt. Starting in cold water and discarding the first boil also helps.
rows.cause: Joint not soaked, or modern gammon particularly well-cured

rows.fix: Replace up to half the milk with the gammon cooking liquid for a more savoury depth. The cooking liquid has absorbed the aromatics and the gentle salt from the joint.
rows.cause: Sauce made with all milk and no cooking liquid
rows.symptom: Sauce tastes flat despite plenty of parsley

content.text: Boiled gammon with parsley sauce is a traditional British weekday and Sunday dinner. It is especially common in the north of England and in Wales. The dish is often linked with school dinners, which has kept it out of fashion. But with good gammon and a properly made sauce, it is one of the better things on the British table.
